name: Infringement Default
description: Default infringement scoring template. 10 component questions scored 1-5 to assess whether a product practices a patent's claims.
scoringGuidance: Score each question on a 1-5 scale using the provided anchor definitions.
Always provide 2-3 sentences of reasoning citing specific evidence from the patent claims and product documentation.
Patent claims use abstract, formal language. Product docs use concrete, practical language. See through terminology differences — focus on WHAT the system does, not what it's CALLED.
If the product documentation is thin or vague, score document_quality accordingly but do not let thin docs contaminate your assessment of functional alignment.
Consider necessary implications: if a product describes feature X, what components must exist to support X even if undocumented?

questions.question: Does the product perform the core function(s) described in the patent claims? Assess whether the product's primary capabilities align with what the patent claims describe, regardless of terminology.

anchors.1: No functional relationship. The product operates in a completely different domain or solves an unrelated problem.
anchors.2: Tangential overlap. The product shares the general technology area but does not implement the core claimed method or system.
anchors.3: Partial alignment. The product implements some of the claimed functions but with significant architectural differences or missing core elements.
anchors.4: Strong alignment. The product implements the core method/system described in the claims, though some specifics differ or use alternative approaches.
anchors.5: Direct alignment. The product clearly implements the core claimed invention — the patent reads on what this product does.

questions.reasoningPrompt: Identify the core function(s) from the patent claims and explain how the product's capabilities relate to them.

questions.question: What fraction of the strongest independent claim's elements are practiced by the product? Break down the claim into elements and assess each.

anchors.1: 0-20% of elements practiced. Most claim elements have no counterpart in the product.
anchors.2: 20-40% of elements practiced. A few elements are present but the majority are missing.
anchors.3: 40-60% of elements practiced. About half the elements are present or have functional equivalents.
anchors.4: 60-80% of elements practiced. Most elements are present; only 1-2 elements are missing or unconfirmed.
anchors.5: 80-100% of elements practiced. Essentially all claim elements are present in the product (even if described differently).

questions.reasoningPrompt: List the key elements of the strongest claim and note which are practiced, partially practiced, or missing.

questions.question: Are undocumented claim elements necessarily implied by the product's described architecture? If a product implements feature X, certain supporting components must exist even if not explicitly documented.

anchors.1: No reasonable inferences possible. The missing elements are independent and cannot be implied from documented features.
anchors.2: Weak inference. One could speculate that some elements exist, but there is no strong architectural necessity.
anchors.3: Moderate inference. Some undocumented elements are likely present as part of standard implementations in this domain.
anchors.4: Strong inference. The described architecture necessarily includes most undocumented elements — they are inherent in how such systems work.
anchors.5: Near-certain inference. The product's described features cannot function without the undocumented elements; their existence is a technical necessity.

questions.reasoningPrompt: Identify which undocumented claim elements are necessarily implied by the product's described architecture and explain why.

questions.question: Do the structural patterns (layers, components, data flows, control planes) in the product match the patent's claimed architecture? This is separate from individual element coverage — it assesses overall system design similarity.

anchors.1: Completely different architecture. The product uses a fundamentally different system design.
anchors.2: Some shared patterns. The product has a few similar components but the overall architecture diverges.
anchors.3: Moderate similarity. The product and patent share the same general architectural pattern but differ in key structural aspects.
anchors.4: High similarity. The system design closely mirrors the patent's architecture — same layers, similar components, comparable data flows.
anchors.5: Near-identical architecture. The product's system design matches the patent's claimed architecture in structure, flow, and component relationships.

questions.reasoningPrompt: Compare the patent's architectural claims (system components, data flows, control relationships) with the product's described architecture.

questions.question: Can patent claim terms be mapped to specific terms used in the product documentation? Strong mappings indicate the product and patent are describing the same things with different words.

anchors.1: No mappable terms. The patent and product use completely different vocabularies with no semantic overlap.
anchors.2: Few mappings. Only 1-2 patent terms can be loosely mapped to product terminology.
anchors.3: Moderate mappings. Several patent terms have plausible product counterparts, but some key terms have no clear match.
anchors.4: Strong mappings. Most patent claim terms can be mapped to specific product documentation terms with reasonable confidence.
anchors.5: Comprehensive mappings. Nearly every patent claim term has a clear counterpart in the product documentation.

questions.reasoningPrompt: List specific patent-to-product term mappings (e.g., patent 'flow entries' = product 'routing rules'). Note any unmappable terms.

questions.question: How deep, relevant, and technically detailed is the available product documentation for assessing infringement? This measures documentation quality, not infringement likelihood.

anchors.1: Minimal or irrelevant documentation. Marketing-only content, or documentation covers unrelated product features. Under 2K chars.
anchors.2: Shallow documentation. Brief technical overview with limited implementation detail. 2-5K chars.
anchors.3: Moderate documentation. Covers the product's relevant features but lacks architectural depth or implementation specifics. 5-20K chars.
anchors.4: Good documentation. Detailed technical content covering architecture, features, and some implementation specifics relevant to the patent. 20-100K chars.
anchors.5: Excellent documentation. Comprehensive technical specification with detailed architecture diagrams, protocol descriptions, and implementation details. 100K+ chars.

questions.reasoningPrompt: Assess the documentation's technical depth, relevance to the patent's technology area, and what information is missing.

questions.question: Does the product implement the same industry standards (IEEE, 3GPP, IETF, MPEG, etc.) that the patent addresses? Standards alignment suggests shared technical requirements.

anchors.1: No standards overlap. The patent and product address different standards or neither involves standards.
anchors.2: Tangential standards. The product and patent involve the same standards body but different specifications.
anchors.3: Related standards. The product implements a standard that is in the same family as the patent's target standard.
anchors.4: Same standard. Both the product and patent target the same standard specification.
anchors.5: Standards essential. The patent appears to cover a feature that is mandatory for compliance with a standard the product implements.

questions.reasoningPrompt: Identify any standards referenced in the patent claims and whether the product documentation mentions implementing those same standards.

questions.question: Could infringement be established solely from this documentation? Consider whether the docs provide enough detail to construct a claim chart.

anchors.1: No evidence. The documentation provides no information useful for establishing infringement.
anchors.2: Weak evidence. A few claim elements can be mapped but major gaps would require additional sources.
anchors.3: Partial evidence. Some claim elements can be mapped to specific doc passages, but a claim chart would have significant holes.
anchors.4: Strong evidence. Most claim elements can be mapped to specific passages; a preliminary claim chart is feasible.
anchors.5: Comprehensive evidence. The documentation provides enough detail to draft a solid claim chart for at least one independent claim.

questions.reasoningPrompt: Assess whether the documentation provides enough specificity to map claim elements to product features for a claim chart.

questions.question: How many independent claims might be practiced by this product? More claims practiced means broader infringement and stronger leverage.

anchors.1: No claims likely practiced. The product does not appear to practice any independent claim.
anchors.2: One claim partially practiced. One independent claim has partial overlap but gaps exist.
anchors.3: One claim likely practiced. At least one independent claim appears to be fully practiced.
anchors.4: Multiple claims likely practiced. 2-3 independent claims appear to be practiced.
anchors.5: Broad infringement. Most or all independent claims appear to be practiced, or one method + one system claim are both practiced.

questions.reasoningPrompt: Estimate how many independent claims the product might practice and note which claim types (method, system, apparatus) are implicated.

questions.question: Stepping back from the individual analyses above: would a patent attorney consider this product a viable target for a claim chart and potential infringement assertion?

anchors.1: Not viable. A patent attorney would not pursue this — no meaningful connection between the patent and product.
anchors.2: Unlikely viable. Some surface-level similarity but too many gaps or differences for a credible assertion.
anchors.3: Possibly viable. Enough overlap to warrant deeper investigation but not enough for a confident assertion.
anchors.4: Likely viable. Strong enough connection that a patent attorney would recommend building a claim chart.
anchors.5: Highly viable. Clear candidate for assertion — a patent attorney would prioritize this pairing.

questions.reasoningPrompt: Give your overall professional assessment of whether this patent-product pair merits further infringement analysis.
